name: Pour-over
tagline: Clean, bright, and full of detail
description: The slow, meditative one. Pour-over gives you a clean cup that lets every flavor shine — you'll taste things you'd miss in other methods. It takes a little patience, but it's worth it.
steps: Bring water to 200–205°F. Rinse the paper filter with hot water and discard the rinse water.
Add 20g of medium-fine ground coffee to the dripper.
Start your timer. Pour just enough water (about 40g) to wet all the grounds — this is the bloom. Wait 30 seconds.
Slowly pour the remaining water in gentle circles, keeping the water level steady. Aim to hit 320g total by 2:30.
Let it drain completely. Total brew time should be around 3:00–3:30.
Swirl your cup gently. Take a sip. Notice what you taste.
best_for: Light and medium roasts, washed process beans, single-origin coffees where you want to taste the origin character. Ethiopian florals, Kenyan fruit-forward lots, and Colombian caramels all shine here.
flavor_impact: Highlights clarity, acidity, and delicate flavors. If a bean has floral or fruity notes, pour-over is where you'll find them.

name: French press
description: The no-fuss classic. French press makes a thick, rich cup with plenty of body. It's forgiving if your grind isn't perfect, and it's great for sharing — just make a big batch.
steps: Bring water to 200°F. Add 30g of coarsely ground coffee to the press.
Pour 450g of hot water over the grounds. Make sure all the coffee is wet.
Place the lid on top (don't press yet) and wait 4 minutes.
Press the plunger down slowly and steadily.
Pour immediately — don't let it sit in the press, or it'll keep extracting and turn bitter.
best_for: Medium and dark roasts, natural process beans, anything with chocolate, nut, or caramel notes. Great for Brazilian and Sumatran coffees.
flavor_impact: Emphasizes body and sweetness. The metal mesh filter lets oils through, giving you a heavier mouthfeel than paper-filtered methods.

name: AeroPress
tagline: Versatile, fast, and hard to mess up
description: The experimenter's favorite. AeroPress is quick, portable, and incredibly forgiving — you can make anything from a clean, tea-like cup to a concentrated espresso-style shot. There are hundreds of recipes because everyone has a different favorite.
steps: Place a filter in the cap and rinse with hot water. Attach to the AeroPress chamber.
Add 15g of medium-ground coffee. Set on a sturdy mug.
Pour 200g of water at 195°F. Stir gently for 10 seconds.
Insert the plunger to create a seal (this stops the drip). Wait 1 minute.
Press down slowly and steadily for about 30 seconds. Stop when you hear a hiss.
Dilute with hot water if you want a longer cup, or drink it as-is for intensity.
best_for: Everything, honestly. Light roasts come out clean and bright, dark roasts come out smooth without bitterness. Especially good for travel and single servings.
flavor_impact: Depends on your recipe — lower temp and coarser grind gives a tea-like clarity; higher temp and finer grind gives body and concentration. That's the fun of it.

name: Espresso
description: The deep end. Espresso is coffee at its most concentrated — a small, powerful shot that reveals everything about a bean, good and bad. It takes practice and proper equipment, but nothing else tastes quite like it.
steps: Grind 18g of coffee very fine — it should feel like fine sand.
Distribute the grounds evenly in the portafilter. Tamp with firm, level pressure.
Lock the portafilter in and start the shot. You're aiming for 36g of liquid in 25–30 seconds.
Watch the stream — it should look like warm honey. If it gushes, grind finer. If it barely drips, grind coarser.
Taste it. A good shot balances sweetness, acidity, and a touch of bitterness.
best_for: Medium to dark roasts, blends designed for espresso, natural process beans with chocolate and fruit notes. Some light roast single-origins make stunning espresso if you dial them in.
flavor_impact: Concentrates everything — acidity becomes snappy, sweetness becomes syrupy, body becomes thick. Crema adds a creamy texture on top.

name: Cold brew
description: The patient one. Cold brew steeps overnight and rewards you with an ultra-smooth, naturally sweet concentrate. It's mellow, low-acid, and perfect for hot days or for people who find hot coffee too intense.
steps: Grind 100g of coffee extra coarse — like raw sugar.
Combine with 800g of room-temperature water in a large jar. Stir to make sure all grounds are wet.
Cover and leave at room temperature or in the fridge for 12–24 hours. Longer = stronger.
Strain through a fine mesh strainer or cheesecloth. Strain a second time if you want extra clarity.
Dilute the concentrate 1:1 with water or milk. Pour over ice.
Keeps in the fridge for up to two weeks.
best_for: Medium and dark roasts, chocolatey and nutty beans, natural process coffees. Brazilian and Colombian coffees make especially smooth cold brew.
flavor_impact: Strips away most acidity and bitterness, leaving sweetness and body. Delicate floral notes won't survive cold brew — this method favors bold, simple flavors.

name: Moka pot
description: The Italian staple. A Moka pot makes a strong, concentrated brew that's somewhere between drip and espresso. It's dramatic (steam pressure!), affordable, and makes your kitchen smell incredible.
ratio: Fill the basket, fill the water to the valve
steps: Fill the bottom chamber with hot water up to the safety valve.
Fill the filter basket with medium-fine ground coffee. Level it off — don't tamp.
Assemble the pot and place on medium heat. Leave the lid open so you can watch.
When coffee starts flowing into the upper chamber, it should be a steady, honey-colored stream.
When you hear sputtering and the stream turns pale, remove from heat immediately. Wrap the base in a cold, wet towel to stop extraction.
Pour and enjoy — it's strong, so add hot water if you want an Americano-style cup.
best_for: Medium to dark roasts, traditional blends, and beans with chocolate, caramel, and spice notes. Great for Italian and Brazilian coffees.
flavor_impact: Produces a concentrated, full-bodied cup with some crema. More intense than drip but less pressurized than true espresso. Highlights sweetness and body over delicate acidity.
